paper.title: Molecular mechanism of non-coding RNAs-mediated radiosensitivity regulation in colorectal cancer

entities.name: PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway

entities.name: Molecular mechanism of non-coding RNAs-mediated radiosensitivity regulation in colorectal cancer

evidence_entities.text: Colorectal cancer (CRC) ranks as the third most diagnosed malignancy globally, with more than 1.92 million (9.6%) new cases and 903859 (9.3%) deaths reported in 2022, making it the second leading cause of cancer-related mortality worldwide
Advanced CRC has a dismal prognosis, with a 5-year survival rate of 10% for metastatic cases compared with 90% for localized disease
poor sensitivity to RT remains a critical barrier in the clinical management of CRC, with up to 30% of patients developing tumor recurrence due to intrinsic or acquired radioresistance
ncRNAs act as pivotal regulators of CRC radiosensitivity by controlling genomic stability maintenance, DNA damage repair (DDR), and cell cycle arrest
miR-141-3p directly targets the oncogenic lncRNA distal-less homeobox 6 antisense 1. Destabilizing its structure and promoting its degradation results in increased CRC radiosensitivity
MiR-630 increases radiosensitivity by targeting BCL2-like 2, an antiapoptotic BCL-2 family member, and TP53 regulating kinase, thereby impairing DNA repair and promoting apoptosis

HOX antisense intergenic RNA drives radioresistance by stabilizing the ataxia-telangiectasia-mutated and Rad3-related protein-ATR-interacting protein complex to sustain DDR and by sponging miR-93 to upregulate the expression of the autophagy-related protein autophagy-related protein 12
circ_0124554 sponges miR-1184 to derepress LIM and SH3 protein 1, a protumorigenic factor stabilized by methyltransferase-like 3-mediated N6-methyladenosine (m6A) methylation
